title: Perfect competition in the short run and long run
description: Practice what you've learned about perfect competition in the short run and the long run, including the firm's shut down rule and entering and exiting an industry.
topic_path: Production, cost, and the perfect competition model
Perfect competition in the short run and long run

assessment_items.question_content:
**If a firm in a perfectly competitive market chooses its profit-maximizing quantity, which of the following MUST be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $P=MC$
$MR<P$
$P>ATC$
$MR>MC$
$MC>ATC$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following MUST be true for a firm in a perfectly competitive market in the short run and in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Price equals marginal cost.
Firms produce the cost-efficient quantity.
Firms earn positive economic profit.
Firms make losses.
Firms charge prices higher than the average total cost.

assessment_items.question_content:
**If a firm produces the quantity where the private marginal cost of the last unit produced equals the private marginal benefit of the last good consumed, which of the following must also be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The quantity produced is allocatively efficient.
The firm is cost-efficient.
All firms are earning a profit
All firms are making losses
The market is in long-run equilibrium

assessment_items.question_content:
**If a market is allocatively efficient, what must be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The price of a good equals the marginal cost of the last item produced.
Price is greater than the average total cost of the last item produced.
Price equals the minimum average total cost.
Marginal revenue equals marginal cost.
Total revenue is less than the total cost

assessment_items.question_content:
Fried Breads Revisited is a food truck in a perfectly competitive industry. Its total cost of producing $100$ donuts is $\$300$ and the market price for a donut is $\$4$. 

**If this firm is representative of a typical firm in this market, which of the following can be inferred?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
If the total cost of producing $100$ donuts is $\$300$ and the price of a donut is $\$4$, then this firm is earning an economic profit of $\$100$:

$\begin{align}\text{Economic Profit} &= \text{Total revenue} - \text{Total cost}\\\\
& = (\$4 \times 100) - \$300\\\\
& = \$400 - \$300\\\\
& =\$100\end{align}$
A perfectly competitive firm can sell as much as it wants at the market price, so it has no incentive to lower the price below the market price.
If firms are making profits, more firms will enter in the long run.

choices.content: Firms will enter this market in the long run.
There is no incentive for firms to enter or exit this market.
This firm should lower its price to increase its sales.
This firm is earning economic losses.
The marginal cost of the $100^{\text{th}}$ unit is greater than $\$4$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The market for happy face stickers is perfectly competitive, and the market price for these stickers is greater than the average total cost (ATC) of a typical firm. 

**Which of the following is true about this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Firms are earning positive economic profits and more firms will enter this industry.
Firms are earning zero economic profits and the market is in long-run equilibrium
Firms are producing a cost-efficient quantity
Firms are producing an allocatively inefficient quantity and firms will exit this industry.
Firms are charging prices higher than the marginal cost of producing stickers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes the conditions that exist in a perfectly competitive market in the *short run*, and which are associated with firms leaving the market in the *long run*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $P<ATC$, $P=MC$, allocative efficiency
$P=ATC$, $P>MC$, productive efficiency
$P=ATC$, $P=MC$, productive efficiency and allocative efficiency
$P<ATC$, $P>MC$, allocative efficiency
$P>ATC$, $P=MC$, productive efficiency

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the average total cost (ATC), marginal cost (MC), and average variable cost (AVC) cost curves for a typical firm in a perfectly competitive market.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What price leads to firms entering this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three curves are displayed. The upward-sloping curve labeled M C starts low, increases sharply, and intersects the A T C and A V C curves at their respective minimum points. The U-shaped curve labeled A T C has its minimum point above price level P 3. The U-shaped curve labeled A V C has its minimum point below price level P 4 and to the left of the A T C curve. Horizontal lines extend from price levels P 1, P 2, P 3, P 4, and P 5 to the Y-axis, showing the cost at different levels.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the average total cost (ATC), marginal cost (MC), and average variable cost (AVC) cost curves for a typical firm in a perfectly competitive market.

[[☃ image 1]]

**At which price is there no incentive for firms to enter or exit this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three curves are displayed. The upward-sloping curve labeled M C starts low, increases sharply, and intersects the A T C and A V C curves at their respective minimum points. The U-shaped curve labeled A T C has its minimum point above price level P 3. The U-shaped curve labeled A V C has its minimum point below price level P 4 and to the left of the A T C curve. assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the average total cost (ATC), marginal cost (MC), and average variable cost (AVC) curves for a typical firm in a perfectly competitive market.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which price is associated with the firm producing in the short run, but exiting the industry in the long run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled Cost, in dollars, and a horizontal axis labeled Quantity. Three curves are displayed. The upward-sloping curve labeled M C starts low, increases sharply, and intersects the A T C and A V C curves at their respective minimum points. The U-shaped curve labeled A T C has its minimum point above price level P 3. The U-shaped curve labeled A V C has its minimum point below price level P 4 and to the left of the A T C curve. Horizontal lines extend from price levels P 1, P 2, P 3, P 4, and P 5 to the Y-axis, showing the cost at different levels.

choices.content: $P_b$
$P_c$
$P_d$
$P_e$
$P_a$

assessment_items.question_content:
Occam’s Razors is a typical firm in a perfectly competitive market. Its total revenue from selling $1000$ razors is $\$2500$ and its variable costs are $\$2000$.

**If the market for razors is in long-run equilibrium, which of the following can be inferred based on the above information?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Firms earn zero profits in long-run equilibrium in a perfectly competitive market.

Profits equal zero when total cost equals total revenue, so this firm’s total costs must be $\$2500$. 
A perfectly competitive firm’s marginal cost equals average total cost in long-run equilibrium. 
If total costs are $\$2500$, the average total cost is 

$\dfrac{\$2500}{1000} = \$2.50$, 

so marginal cost must also be $\$2.50$.

choices.content: The marginal cost of a razor is $\$2.50$ and the average fixed cost is $\$0.50$
The price of a razor is $\$3$ and the marginal cost of a razor is $\$3$.
Average total cost will decrease if the firm increases output
Average total cost will decrease if the firm decreases output
The fixed costs of production are $\$500$ and marginal revenue is $\$2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Glasses, Inc. is a typical firm producing drinking glasses in a perfectly competitive industry. Like other firms in this industry, it's currently making negative economic profits.

**What will happen to the number of firms in the market and the price of this good when this industry returns to long-run equilibrium?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the number of firms decreases; price increases
the number of firms increases; price decreases
the number of firms decreases; price decreases
The number of firms doesn’t change; price increases
The number of firms increases; price doesn’t change

assessment_items.question_content:
Eggs are a normal good sold in a perfectly competitive market that is currently in long-run equilibrium. 

**How will price, output, and profit change if consumer incomes increase?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: price increases; output increases; profit increases
price increases; no change in output; no change in profit
price increases; no change in output; profit increases.
price decreases; output increases; profit decreases
price decreases; output decreases; profit increases
